_general: Carbonized by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in AD 79 and buried in the Villa of the Papyri at Herculaneum — the only library to survive from classical antiquity. Recovered in excavations begun in 1752; too fragile to open physically, it is read non-invasively via X-ray tomography and virtual unwrapping.

tiles.label: with recovered text
tiles.sub: Greek read; first Latin in progress

timeline.title: Naples reveal (press conference)
timeline.blurb: Results across Scrolls 1, 4, 5 and PHerc 0139 to be presented at the papyrology conference in Naples.

timeline.title: Scroll 1 ink predictions refreshed
timeline.blurb: New ink-detection passes on the 2.4µm Paris 4 data.

timeline.title: PHerc 0139 title recovered
timeline.blurb: Identified as Philodemus, On the Gods, Book 8. ~20% unrolled; 1µm slabs give real legibility gains.

timeline.title: Scroll 4 banner read
timeline.blurb: Full-scroll ink banner read end to end — the first scroll read essentially in its entirety.

timeline.title: Scroll 5 ~70% unrolled
timeline.blurb: A major virtual-unrolling milestone (a ~2.5 m surface trace), though ink legibility remains limited.

timeline.title: 2023 Grand Prize
timeline.blurb: First substantial passages of Scroll 1 read — 15+ columns of Greek Epicurean prose.

timeline.title: First Letters
timeline.blurb: First ink letters detected inside an unopened scroll (Scroll 1) — proof the method works.

scrolls.desc:
PHerc. Paris 4 is a rolled Herculaneum papyrus from the [Institut de France](https://www.institutdefrance.fr/en/home/), carbonized during the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It was scanned at the [Diamond Light Source](https://www.diamond.ac.uk) in Oxfordshire, UK.

This is the scroll for which we have by far the most segments, where the [first letters](https://scrollprize.org/firstletters) were discovered and the [2023 Grand Prize](https://scrollprize.org/grandprize) was claimed.
scrolls.repository: Institut de France, Paris
scrolls.note: Scroll 1. The first scroll from which substantial continuous Greek text was recovered (2023 Vesuvius Challenge Grand Prize) — Epicurean philosophy attributed to Philodemus. Rescanned at ESRF at 2.4µm in 2026.

content.summary: Scroll 1 — the flagship. Substantial continuous Greek text recovered from the banner and body; site of the 2023 First Letters and Grand Prize.
content.caveat: The title region shows no detectable ink (possibly a different ink; the top rows are physically missing).

readings.status: Substantial continuous Greek text recovered; ink clearly legible at 2.4µm.

images.cap: Ink prediction overlaid on the 3D papyrus surface (2.4µm)

scrolls.desc:
PHerc. 1667 is a rolled Herculaneum papyrus from the [Biblioteca Nazionale di Napoli](https://www.bnnonline.it/it/121/officina-dei-papiri-ercolanesi), carbonized during the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It was scanned at the [Diamond Light Source](https://www.diamond.ac.uk) in Oxfordshire, UK.

It is similar in size to PHerc. 332 (Scroll 3).
scrolls.repository: Biblioteca Nazionale di Napoli ‘Vittorio Emanuele III’ (Officina dei Papiri Ercolanesi)
scrolls.note: Scroll 4. Read at 2.4µm; a full-scroll ink 'banner' was recovered.

content.summary: Scroll 4 — full-scroll ink banner read end to end, with good legibility outside damaged columns. The first scroll read essentially in its entirety.
content.caveat: Some columns are damaged; a few words remain unresolved even at 2µm and start to appear at 1µm.

scrolls.desc:
PHerc. 172 is a rolled Herculaneum papyrus from the [Bodleian Library](https://www.bodleian.ox.ac.uk/home) in Oxford, carbonized during the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It was scanned at the [Diamond Light Source](https://www.diamond.ac.uk) in Oxfordshire, UK.

It is similar in size to PHerc. Paris 4 (Scroll 1).
scrolls.repository: Biblioteca Nazionale di Napoli ‘Vittorio Emanuele III’ (Officina dei Papiri Ercolanesi)
scrolls.note: Scroll 5. ~70% geometrically unrolled by November 2025 (a 2.5 m trace of a ~7 m banner).

content.summary: Scroll 5 — ~70% geometrically unrolled (a 2.5 m surface trace); text recovered from the unrolled surface.

readings.status: ~70% geometrically unrolled (Nov 2025, a 2.5 m surface trace); text recovered from the unrolled surface (7.9µm).

images.cap: Ink detection across five consecutive windings (71–75) of the unrolled surface (7.9µm)
Ink detection on a single unrolled winding (w087, 7.9µm)

scrolls.repository: Biblioteca Nazionale di Napoli ‘Vittorio Emanuele III’ (Officina dei Papiri Ercolanesi)
scrolls.note: Title recovered: Philodemus, On the Gods, Book 8. ~20% unrolled; legibility still poor.

content.work: Philodemus, On the Gods (Περὶ θεῶν), Book 8
content.summary: Title recovered — a work of Philodemus, On the Gods, Book 8. About 20% unrolled; legibility is poor, and 1µm slabs give real reading gains over 2µm.
content.caveat: ~20% unrolled; ongoing work by M. D'Angelo / Marc on improving legibility.

scrolls.desc:
PHerc. 332 is a rolled Herculaneum papyrus from the [Biblioteca Nazionale di Napoli](https://www.bnnonline.it/it/121/officina-dei-papiri-ercolanesi), carbonized during the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It was scanned at the [Diamond Light Source](https://www.diamond.ac.uk) in Oxfordshire, UK.

It is a smaller scroll known as a *midollo* (marrow), a remnant from physical unwrapping of the larger original. Its small size enabled scanning at extremely high resolution (3.24um).

content.summary: Segmented; early ink-detection results. No continuous text recovered yet.

scrolls.desc:
PHerc. Paris 1 Fr 39 is a fragment of a Herculaneum papyrus, carbonized during the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It was scanned at the [Diamond Light Source](https://www.diamond.ac.uk) in Oxfordshire, UK.

Originally held back for automated scoring in the [Kaggle ink detection competition](https://kaggle.com/competitions/vesuvius-challenge-ink-detection/), this fragment has since been released.

See also: [EduceLab-Scrolls paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.02084), [EduceLab 2023 Info Sheet](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1I6JNrR6A9pMdANbn6uAuXbcDNwjk8qZ2/view?usp=sharing).
scrolls.repository: Institut de France, Paris

scrolls.desc:
PHerc. 51 Cr 4 Fr 8 is a fragment of a Herculaneum papyrus, carbonized during the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It was scanned at the [Diamond Light Source](https://www.diamond.ac.uk) in Oxfordshire, UK.

See also: [EduceLab-Scrolls paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.02084), [EduceLab 2023 Info Sheet](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1I6JNrR6A9pMdANbn6uAuXbcDNwjk8qZ2/view?usp=sharing).
scrolls.repository: Biblioteca Nazionale di Napoli ‘Vittorio Emanuele III’ (Officina dei Papiri Ercolanesi)

scrolls.desc:
PHerc. 1667 Cr 1 Fr 3 is a fragment from the same scroll as PHerc. 1667 (Scroll 4), from the [Biblioteca Nazionale di Napoli](https://www.bnnonline.it/it/121/officina-dei-papiri-ercolanesi). It was partially opened in 1987 via the Oslo method. It was scanned at the [Diamond Light Source](https://www.diamond.ac.uk) in Oxfordshire, UK.

Listed on [Chartes.it](https://www.chartes.it/index.php?r=document/view&id=1691).

See also: [EduceLab-Scrolls paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.02084), [EduceLab 2023 Info Sheet](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1I6JNrR6A9pMdANbn6uAuXbcDNwjk8qZ2/view?usp=sharing).
